---
title: "Inventing Climate Finance"
subtitle: "From Incremental Costs to Strategic Ambiguity"
author: "Minh Ha-Duong (CNRS, CIRED)"
date: "May 2026"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    width: 1600
    height: 900
    footer: "ESHET-HES Joint Conference, Nice 2026"
    logo: ""
bibliography: bibliography/main.bib
---

## {background-color="#1a1a2e"}

::: {.r-fit-text}
How did **climate finance** become countable?
:::

::: {.notes}
Opening hook. The audience is historians of economic thought.
They know Pigou, Coase, Nordhaus. They do NOT know Rio markers.
:::

## The Baku number

> In November 2024, negotiators agreed that developed countries should
> mobilise **$300 billion per year** for climate action in the developing world.

- No institution holds this sum
- No ledger records it
- No methodology for counting it commands consensus

. . .

The number was produced by applying **accounting conventions** that are
themselves the object of bitter dispute.

::: {.notes}
This is the same opening as the paper. Let it land.
:::

## A missing chapter in the HET of climate economics

| Existing HET | This paper |
|---|---|
| Nordhaus, Stern, IAMs [@pottier2016] | Accounting categories |
| Optimisation | Commensuration [@espeland_stevens1998] |
| "What should the carbon price be?" | "Does this dollar count?" |
| Model-building | Category-making |

. . .

**Conference theme fit**: economists under pressure to produce
*usable categories*, not *correct models*

::: {.notes}
Position the contribution. The audience needs to see why this is HET,
not just policy studies. The Espeland & Stevens reference signals STS literacy.
:::

## Three acts

![](figures/fig_bars.png){width="80%"}

::: {.notes}
The figure shows publication volume. Three acts visible:
I. Before (1990-2006), II. Crystallization (2007-2014), III. Disputes (2015-2024)
:::

---

## Act I: Before Climate Finance (1990--2006)

> The developed country Parties [...] shall provide **new and additional**
> financial resources to meet the agreed full costs [...] needed by the
> developing country Parties to meet the agreed full **incremental costs**
> of implementing measures [...] (UNFCCC 1992, Article 4.3)

. . .

Three disconnected traditions, no unified object:

1. **Environmental economics**: externalities, carbon pricing, Coase/Pigou
2. **Development finance**: ODA, aid effectiveness, conditionality
3. **Financial economics**: risk, returns, portfolio theory

::: {.notes}
Emphasise the legal precision of Article 4.3 — it will contrast with the
deliberate ambiguity that replaces it.
:::

## Act II: Crystallization (2007--2014)

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Key events:**

- Bali 2007: negotiations mandate
- Copenhagen 2009: $100bn pledge
- Cancun 2010: Green Climate Fund
- OECD Rio markers: Corfee-Morlot's
  statistical infrastructure
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**What happened:**

- Three traditions converge into one object
- Accounting categories replace legal precision
- The efficiency--accountability divide crystallises
:::
::::

. . .

Corpus evidence: structural break at **2007--2009** (endogenous,
embedding-based detection), not at 2015 or 2021.

::: {.notes}
The key claim: crystallization happened at Copenhagen, not Paris.
The computational evidence corroborates the historical narrative.
:::

## The two poles

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
### Efficiency pole
- Market failure → catalytic public finance
- Leverage, mobilisation, de-risking
- "How to attract private capital?"
- Stern, IFC, Climate Policy Initiative
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
### Accountability pole
- Distributive justice → obligation
- Additionality, grant equivalence, equity
- "Does this dollar really count?"
- Michaelowa, Roberts & Weikmans, Oxfam
:::
::::

. . .

Both poles need each other. The tension is **constitutive** of the field,
not a problem to be resolved.

::: {.notes}
This is the core analytical claim. Present it as a structural feature,
not a normative judgment.
:::

## Act III: Disputes within stable categories (2015--2024)

Four controversies, all fought within categories established in Act II:

| Controversy | Efficiency framing | Accountability framing |
|---|---|---|
| Concessional loans | Leverage, budget-neutral | Grant equivalent, inflated |
| Rio markers | Self-reporting, flexible | Over-reporting, no verification |
| Mobilised private finance | Attribution, multiplier | Double-counting, additionality |
| ODA boundary | Climate-development synergy | Climate relabelling of aid |

. . .

Paris 2015 changed **volume**, not **structure**.

::: {.notes}
The point: nothing structurally new since Copenhagen.
Paris accelerated but did not reorganise the field.
:::

## Strategic ambiguity

From Article 4.3's legal precision:

> "new and additional financial resources [...] agreed full incremental costs"

. . .

To deliberately vague categories:

> "climate finance" (no agreed definition, no agreed methodology, no agreed baseline)

. . .

Ambiguity was not a **deficiency** to be repaired but a **political resource**
exploited by all sides.

::: {.notes}
The punchline. This is what the HET audience should take away.
:::

## Computational corroboration

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- ~30,000 works, 7 sources
- Multilingual (EN, FR, ZH, JA, DE)
- Sentence-transformer embeddings
- Endogenous periodisation
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](figures/fig_composition.png){width="100%"}
:::
::::

. . .

The periodisation and argument rest on the **institutional record**.
The computational patterns **corroborate** the historical narrative
rather than generating it.

::: {.notes}
Keep this brief. The audience cares about the history, not the method.
One slide is enough.
:::

## For discussion

1. What other economic objects were built through **accounting** rather than
   **modelling**? (GDP, inflation, poverty lines, ESG scores...)

2. Does "economists under pressure" always produce **strategic ambiguity**,
   or is climate finance a special case?

3. Is the efficiency--accountability divide specific to climate, or does it
   reproduce a deeper structure in development economics?

::: {.notes}
Open questions for the Q&A. These connect to the conference theme and
invite the audience to bring their own cases.
:::

## Thank you {background-color="#1a1a2e"}

::: {.r-fit-text}
Minh Ha-Duong (CNRS, CIRED)
:::

Paper submitted to *Oeconomia* (under review)

Data & code: [doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19097045](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19097045)

::: {.notes}
Final slide. Keep on screen during Q&A.
:::
